title: Implement Function from Spec
description: Generate a single, well-tested function from a precise specification.
content:
Implement a {{language}} function named `{{name}}` that satisfies this specification:

{{spec}}

Requirements:
- Handle all edge cases explicitly (empty input, nulls, boundaries, overflow).
- No external dependencies unless I name them.
- Add a short docstring describing parameters, return value, and thrown errors.
- Follow idiomatic style and naming for {{language}}.

After the implementation, list the edge cases you handled and any assumptions you made.

title: Add Feature to Existing Code
description: Extend a codebase with a new feature while preserving existing behavior and conventions.
content:
I want to add the following feature: {{feature}}

Before writing code:
1. Identify the files and functions that must change, and explain why.
2. Describe how you'll keep existing behavior intact (and which tests prove it).
3. Match the project's existing patterns, naming, and error-handling style.

Then implement the change as a minimal, reviewable diff. Call out any place where the existing code made you choose between two reasonable approaches.

title: Explain This Code
description: Get a clear, layered explanation of an unfamiliar piece of code.
content:
Explain the following code:

```
{{code}}
```

Structure your answer as:
1. One sentence: what it does overall.
2. A step-by-step walk-through of the control flow.
3. Any non-obvious details — performance characteristics, side effects, edge cases, or subtle bugs.
4. What a caller needs to know to use it correctly.

Assume I'm a competent engineer new to this specific code.

title: Convert Pseudocode to Code
description: Translate an algorithm or pseudocode into working, idiomatic code.
content:
Translate the following pseudocode/algorithm into idiomatic {{language}}:

{{pseudocode}}

- Preserve the algorithm's complexity; note the Big-O of the result.
- Use clear names; don't leave single-letter variables unless conventional.
- Add input validation where a real caller could pass bad data.
- Include a couple of usage examples.

title: Implement an API Client
description: Write a typed client for a REST or RPC API from its docs.
content:
Write a {{language}} client for this API:

{{api_description}}

Requirements:
- Strong types for every request and response shape.
- Centralized error handling that distinguishes network, 4xx, and 5xx failures.
- Timeouts and a single, configurable retry policy for idempotent calls.
- No secrets in code — read credentials from configuration/env.
- A short usage example for the two most common calls.

title: Add Robust Error Handling
description: Harden a function or module against failure with deliberate error handling.
content:
Review the code below and add robust error handling:

```
{{code}}
```

For each failure mode, decide deliberately: validate-and-reject, retry, fall back, or propagate. Then:
- Validate inputs at the boundary; fail fast with actionable messages.
- Never swallow errors silently — log or rethrow with context.
- Distinguish expected failures (return/throw typed errors) from bugs (let them surface).
- Keep the happy path readable; don't drown it in try/catch.

Show the revised code and a one-line rationale per change.

title: Write a CLI Command
description: Build a command-line subcommand with argument parsing and good UX.
content:
Create a CLI command `{{command}}` in {{language}} that: {{behavior}}

Requirements:
- Parse flags and positional args with clear `--help` output.
- Validate arguments and exit with a non-zero code and a useful message on error.
- Support `--json` for machine-readable output where it makes sense.
- Be quiet on success by default; add `--verbose` for detail.
- Handle Ctrl+C cleanly.

title: Concurrency-Safe Implementation
description: Implement logic that is correct under concurrent access.
content:
Implement the following with correct behavior under concurrency: {{task}}

In {{language}}:
- Identify the shared state and the exact invariants that must hold.
- Choose the simplest correct synchronization (immutability > message passing > locks).
- Avoid deadlocks and races; explain why your choice is free of both.
- Note the performance trade-off you made.

Provide the code plus a short paragraph on how it behaves under contention.
